$$r = 1 + \text{cos} \theta$$

Substitute $$-\theta$$ for $$\theta$$:
$$r = 1 + \text{cos}(-\theta)$$

Using the trigonometric identity that cosine is an even function,  $$\text{cos}(-\theta) = \text{cos}(\theta)$$, we get:
$$r = 1 + \text{cos}\theta$$

Since the resulting equation is identical to the original equation, the graph **is symmetric with respect to the x-axis**.

### 2. Test for Symmetry with Respect to the y-axis (the line $$\theta = \frac{\pi}{2}$$)

To test for symmetry with respect to the y-axis, we replace $$\theta$$ with $$\pi - \theta$$ in the equation. If the resulting equation is equivalent to the original, the graph is symmetric with respect to the y-axis.

Original equation:
$$r = 1 + \text{cos} \theta$$

Substitute $$\pi - \theta$$ for $$\theta$$:
$$r = 1 + \text{cos}(\pi - \theta)$$

Using the trigonometric identity  $$\text{cos}(\pi - \theta) = -\text{cos}(\theta)$$, we get:
$$r = 1 - \text{cos}\theta$$

Since the resulting equation, $$r = 1 - \text{cos}\theta$$, is not equivalent to the original equation, $$r = 1 + \text{cos}\theta$$, the graph is **not symmetric with respect to the y-axis**.

### 3. Test for Symmetry with Respect to the Origin (the Pole)

To test for symmetry with respect to the origin, we replace $$r$$ with $$-r$$ in the equation. If the resulting equation is equivalent to the original, the graph is symmetric with respect to the origin.

Original equation:
$$r = 1 + \text{cos} \theta$$

Substitute $$-r$$ for $$r$$:
$$-r = 1 + \text{cos}\theta$$

Multiplying by -1, we get:
$$r = -1 - \text{cos}\theta$$

Since the resulting equation, $$r = -1 - \text{cos}\theta$$, is not equivalent to the original equation, the graph is **not symmetric with respect to the origin**.

### Conclusion

The graph of the polar equation $$r = 1 + \text{cos} \theta$$ is **symmetric with respect to the x-axis** only.
